author: @ShikharMisra94
text:
Hello Robert.. I really enjoy watching your videos as I get to learn a lot from them.. 
Right now, I am working on liquid phase exfoliation to produce graphene and planning to make a water filter from the exfoliated graphene.. It would be great if you can suggest some ideas to make water filter from graphene which I have in a powder form.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Shikhar Misra My suggestion is not to bother - the problem of adhering the graphene is difficult especially when better methods exist

replies.author: @ShikharMisra94
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith I am actually preparing bulk graphene using kitchen blender in NMP solvent. I have separated it using vacuum filtration after centrifuging it and was wondering how to test it for water filtration.. One idea that struck me recently was to use a tea bag.. It would be great of you can suggest some other better idea..thanks for your help

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Shikhar Misra sounds like a plan mate good luck with it

author: @drrobindeans
text: Hello Robert... thanks for your great videos.. learned a lot and wished I had more time... My son is thinking of making a graphene impregnated chitosan matrix for his grade 8 science fair.. He plans to test it out as a water filter . Any thoughts on how he could do this ? Thanks a lot . Rob

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a good idea mate - his best bet is to mix graphene oxide and chitosan and hydrothermally carbonise it for 18 hours or so at 180 degrees centigrade - he can do this in a normal cooker

replies.author: @drrobindeans
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Rob... You are the best .. thanks a lot ..

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It has a degree of conductivity - not great because of the grain boundaries but activated carbon is non conductive - apart from that Raman and SEM will confirm

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It has a degree of conductivity - not great because of the grain boundaries but activated carbon is non conductive - apart from that Raman and SEM will confirm

author: @SiliconM4trix
text:
Okay, that makes sense. It would most likely result in (at best) activated charcoal coated sand. That said, how are you confirming the presence of graphene/graphene-oxide on the sand, as opposed to activated charcoal? Aside from using an STM, is there some easily observable property that can be used to distinguish between graphene(/oxide), graphite, and charcoal coatings?
Thanks

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: i don't know - but I doubt it as a process of graphetisation has to be gone through and I don't think the hydrothermal condition will do this - but it is worth a go. If you try it let me know

author: @SiliconM4trix
text: Would it be possible to process the tar coated sand via hydrothermal carbonization then up the concentration of sulfuric acid to activate it? Or maybe start with less dilute sulfuric acid and kill two birds with one stone?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: anything that would dissolve the asphalt - unfortunately that is toluene and that is about it for easy to get hold of solvents

author: @buensomeritano1755
text: What other solvents, than toluene could be used?

text: in brief - it's quicker, uses less material to do the same job and clears more contaminants out of the water - for a fuller answer google it.
